description.en: A class is not a blueprint frozen in syntax — it is itself an object, created at runtime by its metaclass (default `type`). Learn how `type(name, bases, namespace)` builds classes dynamically, how a metaclass hooks into class-creation time to inspect or modify a class as it's defined, the real jobs metaclasses do well — and the famous warning that almost every case you imagine is better served by `__init_subclass__` or a class decorator.

story_variants.en:
## A Class Is an Object Too

You already know that in Python *everything is an object* — integers, lists, functions. Here is the part that bends people's brains: **a class is an object as well.** When you write `class Dog: ...`, you don't just describe a type; you *create an object* named `Dog` that lives on the heap like any other value. You can put it in a list, pass it to a function, give it attributes.

And every object has a type. So what is the type of a *class*?

```python
class Dog:
    pass

print(type(Dog))   # <class 'type'>
print(type(42))    # <class 'int'>
print(type(int))   # <class 'type'>
```

`42` is an instance of `int`. `Dog` is an instance of `type`. **`type` is the metaclass** — the class whose instances are themselves classes. A metaclass is to a class what a class is to an instance.

> **Key idea:** A class is an instance of its metaclass. The default metaclass is `type`. Writing `class Foo:` is really *calling* `type` to manufacture a new class object at runtime.

## `type` Has Two Faces

Called with **one** argument, `type(obj)` tells you an object's type. Called with **three**, `type(...)` *builds a brand-new class* on the spot. These two `Dog` definitions are equivalent:

```python
# The familiar statement form
class Dog:
    legs = 4
    def speak(self):
        return "woof"

# The dynamic, three-argument form — identical result
def speak(self):
    return "woof"

Dog = type("Dog", (), {"legs": 4, "speak": speak})
```

The `class` keyword is *syntactic sugar*. Under the hood the interpreter gathers the three ingredients and hands them to `type`:

| Argument | Name | What it is | In `class Dog(Animal):` |
|----------|------|------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | `name` | the class's `__name__` string | `"Dog"` |
| 2 | `bases` | tuple of parent classes | `(Animal,)` |
| 3 | `namespace` | dict of the class body (methods, attrs) | `{"legs": 4, "speak": <fn>}` |

So `type(name, bases, namespace)` is the literal machinery of class creation. A **metaclass** is simply a callable — almost always a subclass of `type` — that you slot in to run *instead of* `type` at that moment.

## Hooking Class Creation

Define a metaclass by subclassing `type` and overriding `__new__` or `__init__`. Either runs **once, at class-definition time** — not when instances are created:

```python
class Loud(type):
    def __new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace):
        namespace["shout"] = lambda self: name.upper() + "!"
        return super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, namespace)

class Greeter(metaclass=Loud):
    pass

print(Greeter().shout())   # GREETER!
```

When Python sees `metaclass=Loud`, it calls `Loud("Greeter", (), {...})` instead of `type(...)`. `Loud.__new__` receives the same three ingredients, *edits the namespace* (here injecting a `shout` method), and returns the finished class. The metaclass got to inspect and modify the class **as it was being born.**

## What Metaclasses Are Actually Good For

The legitimate, classic uses all share one trait: they need to act on a class *itself*, automatically, the moment it's defined.

- **Auto-registration** — record every subclass into a registry the instant it's declared (plugin systems, serializers, ORM model tables). No manual `register(MyClass)` calls to forget.
- **Enforcing conventions** — reject a class at definition time if it's missing a required method or attribute, turning a runtime surprise into an import-time error.
- **Injecting behavior** — add methods, wrap existing ones, or compute attributes for every class in a hierarchy uniformly.

## ...And the Famous Warning

Metaclasses are powerful, implicit, and easy to over-apply. Tim Peters' advice has aged perfectly:

> *"Metaclasses are deeper magic than 99% of users should ever worry about. If you wonder whether you need them, you don't."*

Since Python 3.6, most cases that *used* to demand a metaclass have a simpler answer:

- **`__init_subclass__`** — a hook on the **base class** that runs whenever it's subclassed. Perfect for registration and validation, with none of the metaclass machinery.
- **Class decorators** — `@something` above a class can transform it after creation. Explicit, composable, and easy to read.

Reach for a real metaclass only when you must control creation across an entire hierarchy in a way those two can't express. Understand them deeply — then usually pick the lighter tool.

What you've gained is the model: `class` is sugar for a call to `type`, every class is an instance of a metaclass, and that creation moment is a hook you can occupy. That single idea demystifies ORMs, plugin registries, and half the "magic" in serious Python frameworks.

children: int / Dog is an instance of  type (the metaclass)
type      is an instance of  type (itself)
class Foo: ... really CALLS type(name, bases, ns)
metaclass = a callable (subclass of type) run at class creation

animation_sequence.title: class is sugar for type(name, bases, namespace)

steps.description: Arg 1 'Dog' -> __name__. Arg 2 () -> no bases. Arg 3 dict -> the class body.
Dog is an instance of `type` -> <class 'type'>. A class is an object.
The namespace dict became the class attributes: prints 4.

left.label: Metaclass(type): controls creation across a whole hierarchy — powerful, implicit

right.label: __init_subclass__ / class decorator: covers most cases — simpler, explicit

description.en: 'If you wonder whether you need a metaclass, you don't.' Reach for __init_subclass__ or a decorator first; use a metaclass only when you truly must hook creation itself.

executionSteps.description: Defining Dog runs RegistryMeta.__init__ once -> Dog records itself.
Defining Cat runs the metaclass again -> Cat auto-registers too.
No manual register() calls — creation-time hook did it all.

output: A class is an object: an instance of its metaclass (default `type`).
`class` is sugar for type(name, bases, namespace).
Metaclasses hook class CREATION — but prefer __init_subclass__ or a decorator.

instruction.en: Build a metaclass that auto-registers every class that uses it. Complete `RegistryMeta(type)` so that defining a class records it into the class-level `registry` dict, keyed by the class's name. Use the `__init__` hook (which runs once, at class-creation time) and store `cls` under `name`. Do NOT register manually — the whole point is that `class Dog(metaclass=RegistryMeta): ...` registers itself automatically. The demo defines Dog and Cat, then prints the sorted registry keys. Expected output: ['Cat', 'Dog']

en: A metaclass's __init__ runs ONCE when a class is defined — not when instances are made. Its `cls` argument is the freshly built class object itself.
Call super().__init__(name, bases, namespace) first so `type` finishes constructing the class normally.
To auto-register, add one line: RegistryMeta.registry[name] = cls — keying the dict by the class name string.
sorted() on a dict iterates its keys, so sorted(RegistryMeta.registry) yields the names alphabetically: ['Cat', 'Dog'].

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert "Dog" in RegistryMeta.registry
assert "Cat" in RegistryMeta.registry
assert RegistryMeta.registry["Dog"] is Dog
assert RegistryMeta.registry["Cat"] is Cat
assert sorted(RegistryMeta.registry) == ["Cat", "Dog"]
# The base type itself must not have been swept into the registry.
assert "type" not in RegistryMeta.registry
assert "object" not in RegistryMeta.registry
assert len(RegistryMeta.registry) == 2

q.en: In Python, what is the type of a class such as `Dog` (defined with a plain `class Dog: ...`)?

en: It has no type; only instances have types
`type` — a class is an instance of its metaclass, which defaults to `type`
`object`, because every class inherits from object
`Dog` is its own type

q.en: What does the three-argument form `type(name, bases, namespace)` do?

en: Returns the type of an existing object
Renames an existing class in place
Creates a brand-new class object dynamically from its name, parent tuple, and body dict
Converts a dict into an instance
Every time an instance of the class is created
Once, at class-definition (creation) time
Only when the module is garbage-collected
Each time a method on the class is called

q.en: For simply registering or validating subclasses, what's usually preferred over writing a metaclass?

en: Always write a metaclass — there is no lighter option
Monkey-patching `type` globally
`__init_subclass__` on the base class, or a class decorator — simpler and more explicit
Using `eval()` on the class body
